planting.note: Plant seeds in fall for natural stratification or plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, fertile to average, loamy or clay loam soil in full sun to partial shade; European hornbeam is remarkably adaptable to a wide range of soil types, tolerating heavy clay soils and moderately wet or dry conditions.

directions: Plant seeds in fall or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, fertile to average, loamy or clay loam soil in full sun to partial shade; European hornbeam is one of the most important and widely planted trees in the European formal garden tradition — valued both as a large specimen tree and as one of the premier hedging and pleaching trees, because its dense, twiggy branching retains dead tan leaves through the winter (marcescence) just like beech, creating a solid, opaque winter hedge. The wood is extraordinarily hard and tough — 'hornbeam' literally means 'hard tree' — historically used for making cogwheels, tool handles, and cutting boards. The oval leaves turn gold in autumn. The fruiting catkins with their distinctive three-lobed involucres (leafy bracts around each nut) are attractive. Exceptionally pollution-tolerant and adaptable to urban conditions. The cultivar 'Fastigiata' (columnar hornbeam) is one of the premier street trees in European cities. An excellent hedge, screen, and formal pleached allee tree for gardens with the space for its imposing mature size.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy soil in partial shade; Japanese hornbeam grows naturally as an understory tree in Japanese forests and is more shade-tolerant and slower-growing than European hornbeam, making it appropriate for smaller garden spaces and partially shaded positions.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy soil in partial to full shade; Japanese hornbeam is the most ornamentally distinctive of all the hornbeams — the relatively large, prominently veined, dark green leaves are deeply ridged with parallel veins and have strongly toothed margins, combined with an outstanding fruiting display of large, hop-like pendulous clusters of closely overlapping, fan-shaped bracts surrounding each tiny nut, creating one of the most ornamental fruiting displays of any hornbeam. The fruiting catkins hang in pendulous strings of tightly packed, flat-winged structures that give the branches a distinctive appearance in summer and autumn. More compact and shade-tolerant than European hornbeam — an excellent choice for smaller gardens and for partially shaded positions under taller trees. Excellent fall color (yellow to orange). An underused but refined and highly ornamental small ornamental tree for shaded garden settings.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown plants in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, rocky or loamy, lean to average soil in full sun; American smoke tree grows naturally on rocky, alkaline, limestone-based, shallow soils in the Ozarks, Edwards Plateau, and southern Appalachians. Dislikes heavy, wet, fertile soils — lean, dry, well-drained conditions produce the best fall color.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown plants in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, rocky or loamy, lean to average soil in full sun; American smoke tree is the native North American species of smoke tree — rarer, larger, and considered by many horticulturists to be superior to the European smoke tree (Cotinus coggygria) for fall color. The large, oval, blue-green leaves turn extraordinary shades of orange, scarlet, and burgundy in autumn, rivaling the best maples for fall color brilliance. Named 'chittamwood' in the Ozarks, where it grows on rocky, limestone outcrops and cedar glades. The smoke-like display in summer (from the fluffy, hair-like styles remaining after flowering) is less showy than the European species but still attractive. Extremely drought-tolerant on dry, rocky, alkaline soils. Much less commonly grown than European smoke tree, despite its superior fall color — an underused native ornamental tree that deserves wider planting in appropriate dry, rocky, well-drained garden settings across the eastern US.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained to dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to lean, rocky, loamy, or sandy soil in full sun; European smoke tree is very drought-tolerant once established and actually produces more vibrant foliage color and a better 'smoke' display in lean, slightly stressed, well-drained to dry soils than in rich, moist, fertile conditions.

directions: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained to dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to lean, rocky, loamy, or sandy soil in full sun; European smoke tree is one of the most widely grown and loved ornamental shrubs in temperate gardens — famous for the extraordinary summer display of its tiny flowers, which are followed by large, loose panicles of fine, hair-like, pinkish-purple styles (the fuzzy threads remaining after the petals fall), creating the effect of a misty 'smoke cloud' surrounding the entire shrub in summer. The purple-leaved cultivars ('Royal Purple', 'Grace', 'Velvet Cloak') are among the most popular shrubs in the garden center trade — the deep wine-purple foliage contrasting with the ethereal pink-purple smoke creates a striking effect through the entire growing season. Fall color in the purple forms is spectacular: scarlet to orange-crimson. Native to southern Europe and central Asia. Extremely adaptable — tolerating dry, lean, rocky, alkaline soils where other flowering shrubs struggle. The purple-leaved forms are ubiquitous in modern garden design for their dramatic foliage season-long effect.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep, loamy soil in partial shade; dove tree grows naturally in cool, moist, mountainous forests of central China and requires adequate moisture, cool conditions, and some shelter from harsh afternoon sun and drying winds. Seeds require double-stratification (2 cold periods) and may take 2 years to germinate.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep, loamy soil in partial shade; dove tree is one of the most spectacular flowering trees in the world, with a legendary history in Western botany — first seen by European plant hunters in Chinese mountain forests and described as a tree covered with fluttering white handkerchiefs or white doves perching in the branches. The 'flowers' (actually a tiny, inconspicuous cluster of flowers) are almost invisible — what creates the stunning display is a pair of very large, unequal, pure white bracts (modified leaves) flanking each flower cluster, ranging from 3 to 7 inches long. When in full bloom in mid-spring, a mature tree covered in hundreds of hanging white bract pairs in a light breeze creates an unforgettable, magical spectacle. The tree requires patience — it typically does not flower until 10+ years from seed. A legendary collector's tree planted in large gardens and arboreta worldwide for this once-yearly flower event. The fruit is a large, olive-like, edible (barely) nutlet. The legendary China plant collector E.H. Wilson nearly died collecting seeds in 1900.
